title: Responsive Calculator
summary: Build a polished calculator with keyboard support, validation and an accessible interface.
brief: Create a calculator that supports addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, decimals, clear, backspace and keyboard input.
requirements: Display the current expression and result
Provide visible focus states and accessible labels

hints: Represent the current calculation as a string and render it after every input.
Use event delegation on the keypad rather than one listener per button.
Keep display logic, input validation and calculation in separate functions.
Avoid raw eval in production code. For this constrained exercise, parse two operands and one operator.

title: Weather Dashboard
summary: Build a city weather dashboard with loading, empty and error states plus a recent-search history.
brief: Create a weather application where users search for a city and receive current conditions plus a short forecast.
requirements: Show explicit loading and error states
Render current temperature, conditions and wind
Render at least three forecast cards
Use a mock-data fallback so the learning project still works without an API

files.index.html: Weather Dashboard

hints: Build the UI state machine first: idle, loading, success and error.
Create one renderWeather(data) function rather than editing the DOM in several places.
Start with MOCK_WEATHER. Add a real endpoint only after the complete user flow works.
Store only successful city names in localStorage and cap history at five entries.

solution.index.html:
FORECAST STUDIO

Weather Dashboard

Three-day outlook

Recent searches
solution.app.js:
const MOCK_WEATHER={London:{temperature:19,condition:"Partly cloudy",wind:14,forecast:[18,20,17]},Mumbai:{temperature:29,condition:"Rain showers",wind:18,forecast:[29,28,30]},Edinburgh:{temperature:15,condition:"Light rain",wind:21,forecast:[14,16,15]}};const form=document.querySelector("#searchForm"),statusEl=document.querySelector("#status"),currentEl=document.querySelector("#current"),forecastEl=document.querySelector("#forecast"),historyEl=document.querySelector("#history");let history=JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem("weather-history")||"[]");function renderHistory(){historyEl.innerHTML=history.map(city=>``).join("")}function saveCity(city){history=[city,...history.filter(x=>x!==city)].slice(0,5);localStorage.setItem("weather-history",JSON.stringify(history));renderHistory()}async function loadWeather(city){statusEl.textContent="Loading weather…";currentEl.classList.add("hidden");forecastEl.innerHTML="";await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,450));const key=Object.keys(MOCK_WEATHER).find(x=>x.toLowerCase()===city.toLowerCase());if(!key){statusEl.textContent="City not found in the learning dataset. Try London, Mumbai or Edinburgh.";return}const data=MOCK_WEATHER[key];statusEl.textContent="";currentEl.classList.remove("hidden");currentEl.innerHTML=`

${key}

${data.condition}

${data.temperature}°C

Wind ${data.wind} km/h

`;forecastEl.innerHTML=data.forecast.map((temp,i)=>`
Day ${i+1}

${temp}°C

`).join("");saveCity(key)}form.addEventListener("submit",e=>{e.preventDefault();loadWeather(document.querySelector("#city").value.trim())});historyEl.addEventListener("click",e=>{const city=e.target.dataset.city;if(city)loadWeather(city)});renderHistory();

title: Personal Expense Tracker
summary: Build a command-line expense tracker with validation, summaries and JSON persistence.
brief: Create an expense tracker that records transactions, lists them, calculates totals by category and saves data to JSON.
requirements: Add an expense with date, category, description and amount
hints: Keep input/output code separate from the core expense functions.
Convert amount to float inside add_expense and raise ValueError for zero or negative values.
Use json.loads only after checking that the file exists and contains text.
Build category totals with a dictionary and dict.get(category, 0).

title: Java Task Manager
summary: Build a layered Java task manager with domain objects, a service and a console interface.
brief: Create a Java task manager where users can add, list and complete tasks.
requirements: Represent tasks with a dedicated Task class
Use a TaskService to manage the collection
Keep console input outside the domain class
hints: Task should own its completion state; TaskService should own the collection.
Use a generated integer ID so callers do not depend on list positions.
Return an unmodifiable copy or new list from listTasks.
Throw IllegalArgumentException for blank titles and missing IDs.

checks.label: Does not expose the mutable list directly

title: Sales Insight Dashboard Dataset
summary: Write a portfolio-grade SQL analysis answering executive sales questions.
brief: Analyse customers, orders and products to identify revenue trends, top products and at-risk regions.
requirements: Compare each region with the overall average

files.analysis.sql:
-- Sales Insight Capstone
-- 1. Document the grain of each source table.
-- 2. Build monthly revenue.
-- 3. Rank products within category.
-- 4. Compare regions with the overall average.
-- 5. Identify customers with falling spend.
files.README.md:
# Sales Insight Capstone

## Business questions

## Data assumptions

## Findings

## Recommendation

hints: Start by defining the grain of customers, orders and order_items.
Aggregate order_items before joining to dimensions when you need one row per order.
Use DENSE_RANK() with PARTITION BY category for product ranking.
Use LAG() to compare a customer’s current period with the previous period.

solution.analysis.sql:
-- Monthly revenue
WITH order_revenue AS (
  SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id, o.order_date,
         SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  GROUP BY o.order_id, o.customer_id, o.order_date
), monthly AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
         SUM(revenue) AS revenue
  FROM order_revenue
  GROUP BY 1
), product_revenue AS (
  SELECT p.category, p.product_name,
         SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN products p ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
  GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
), ranked_products AS (
  SELECT *, DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY category ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS category_rank
  FROM product_revenue
), regional AS (
  SELECT c.region, SUM(r.revenue) AS revenue
  FROM order_revenue r
  JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
  GROUP BY c.region
), customer_month AS (
  SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(revenue) AS revenue
  FROM order_revenue GROUP BY 1,2
), customer_change AS (
  SELECT *, LAG(revenue) OVER(PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY month) AS previous_revenue
  FROM customer_month
)
SELECT * FROM monthly ORDER BY month;

-- Top products by category
SELECT * FROM ranked_products WHERE category_rank <= 3;

-- Regions below average
SELECT region, revenue, AVG(revenue) OVER() AS average_region_revenue
FROM regional
QUALIFY revenue < AVG(revenue) OVER();

-- Customers with falling spend
SELECT * FROM customer_change
WHERE previous_revenue IS NOT NULL AND revenue < previous_revenue;
solution.README.md:
# Sales Insight Capstone

## Grain
- customers: one row per customer
- orders: one row per order
- order_items: one row per order-product line

## Main finding
Monthly revenue should be analysed after order-line revenue is aggregated to avoid accidental multiplication.

## Recommendation
Prioritise declining high-value customer segments and validate regional results against the source-system control total before executive publication.
